title: Today's Tech Brief: A Crash Course in Mountain Bike Suspension (2026)
summary: Mountain bike suspension explained, Google's AI search opt-out, and more

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. Let's dive into some interesting news. First up, if you're like me and enjoy a good mountain bike ride, you might have noticed that the rocks don't get any softer with age. A recent article from Wired explains the basics of mountain bike suspension, and I have to say, it's a crash course I wish I had taken years ago. I mean, who doesn't love a smooth ride?

The article from Wired breaks down the essentials of mountain bike suspension, which is essentially a system designed to absorb bumps and shocks, making your ride smoother. As someone who's getting a bit older and wiser, I appreciate the importance of this tech. No more bruised backsides from unexpected rocks! It's amazing how much of a difference it can make, and I'm tempted to upgrade my bike's suspension ASAP.

Next, Google has been making waves with its AI search features. According to a report from The Verge, the UK's Competition and Markets Authority has ruled that Google must allow publishers to opt out of AI search features. This means website owners can prevent their content from being used to fine-tune Google's AI models. It's an interesting move, and I'll be keeping an eye on how this develops. Will this change the way we interact with search results?

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
